narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, along with your LSAT score of 32, your lower-middle family income background, and your plan to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts whether students like you are likely to pass the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 57.4% chance that you would fail it—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight. The strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your first-year school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your classmates that year, which raised concerns about early momentum in a rigorous field like law. Next, though your undergraduate GPA pulled strongly in the positive direction—suggesting solid college-level preparation—it wasn't quite enough to offset that early dip. Your third-year decile of 2, still near the bottom of your class, added further influence toward the failure side, even if it showed some improvement over first year. Compared to typical students, who average around the middle of their classes in both early and later years, your lower rankings stood out as the biggest hurdles, while your above-average undergrad performance was a bright spot that nearly balanced things. These three elements—your Year 1 and Year 3 deciles as top concerns, with undergrad GPA helping—dominated the model's thinking for your profile. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with your full-time commitment and personal drive as a Hispanic man navigating these paths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the 36.7 average, or focus on programs offering strong academic support to build those decile rankings. You could also seek feedback from undergrad advisors on strengthening early performance patterns, and explore schools with holistic reviews that value your background. With targeted steps, you can reshape this story and reapply stronger.
